title: Panchatantra Moral Fables Chapter 2 (Expansion Batch 2)
source_name: The Panchatantra, translated by Arthur W. Ryder (1925, University of Chicago Press)
revision_note: Panchatantra Expansion Batch 2 (2026-07-23). Selected the 10 next reader stories from src/lib/katha-library.ts (PANCHATANTRA_STORIES + MORE_PANCHATANTRA_STORIES) whose plot I could confirm, paragraph-by-paragraph, against a verbatim Ryder (1925) text fetched from theoceanofstories.blogspot.com (Laura Gibbs, Univ. of Oklahoma course site; explicitly states the underlying Panchatantra stories are public domain, cross-attributed to Ryder 1925 on every page). Each item's 'text' field is Ryder's verbatim translation; 'curated_lesson' is the existing Shoonaya reader paraphrase from katha-library.ts, reproduced here as a separate, clearly labeled layer (source_class curated_lesson / rights_status rights_cleared), not a translation. 'Crows and Owls' (2.8) is the Book 3 frametale, which runs many thousands of words; the 'text' field is a trimmed verbatim excerpt (opening declaration of the feud + the crow spy Live-Strong's fire stratagem + closing moral couplet), with internal cuts marked '[...]'; the full text is at the cited source_url. Two candidate stories were evaluated and explicitly SKIPPED as not confidently matched to Ryder's Panchatantra: 'panchatantra-dove-king-and-net' (The Dove King and the Net) — its plot (a flock of doves lifts a hunter's net together and is freed by a mouse ally) does not match Ryder's 'The Self-Sacrificing Dove' (a lone dove immolates himself for a fowler-guest) or any other titled story in Ryder's captured table of contents; it may derive from the Hitopadesha or another collection, not verified this batch. 'panchatantra-mongoose-and-child' (The Loyal Mongoose) is a confident title/plot match to Ryder Book 5 'The Loyal Mungoose', but no verbatim Ryder text for Book 5 could be located at theoceanofstories.blogspot.com (that blog only transcribed Books 1-4); sourcing Book 5 text (e.g. via sacred-texts.com, archive.org, or shreevatsa.net) is deferred to a future batch. Sanskrit/transliteration intentionally omitted per docs/PANCHATANTRA_CANONICAL_SOURCE_PLAN.md.

content.text: In a certain town lived a Brahman named Friendly who had undertaken the labour of maintaining the sacred fire. One day in the month of February, when a gentle breeze was blowing, when the sky was veiled in clouds and a drizzling rain was falling, he went to another village to beg a victim for the sacrifice, and said to a certain man: "O sacrificer, I wish to make an offering on the approaching day of the new moon. Pray give me a victim." And the man gave him a plump goat, as prescribed in Scripture. This he put through its paces, found it sound, placed it on his shoulder, and started in haste for his own city. Now on the road he was met by three rogues whose throats were pinched with hunger. These, spying the plump creature on his shoulder, whispered together: "Come now! If we could eat that creature, we should have the laugh on this sleety weather. Let us fool him, get the goat, and ward off the cold." So the first of them changed his dress, issued from a by-path to meet the Brahman, and thus addressed that man of pious life: "O pious Brahman, why are you doing a thing so unconventional and so ridiculous? You are carrying an unclean animal, a dog, on your shoulder." At that the Brahman was mastered by anger, and he said: "Are you blind, man, that you impute doghood to a goat?" "O Brahman," said the rogue, "do not be angry. Go where you will." But when he had travelled a little farther, the second rogue met him and said: "Alas, holy sir, alas! Even if this dead calf was a pet, still you should not put it on your shoulder." Then the Brahman spoke in anger: "Are you blind, man? You call a goat a calf." And the rogue said: "Holy sir, do not be angry. I spoke in ignorance. Do as you will." But when he had walked only a little farther through the forest, the third rogue, changing his dress, met him and said: "Sir, this is most improper. You are carrying a donkey on your shoulder... Pray drop this thing, before another sees you." So the Brahman concluded that it was a goblin in quadruped form, threw it on the ground, and made for home, terrified. Meanwhile, the three rogues met, caught the goat, and carried out their plan. "And that is why I say: The strong, deft, clever rascals note, Who robbed the Brahman of his goat."
content.curated_lesson: A Brahmin once received a healthy goat as a gift and carried it home on his shoulders. Three crooks saw him and wanted the goat, but they knew they could not snatch it openly. The first crook came along the road and said, "Revered sir, why are you carrying a dirty dog on your shoulders?" The Brahmin was offended. "This is a goat, not a dog," he replied and walked on. After some distance, the second crook appeared and said with surprise, "O learned one, why would a Brahmin carry a dead calf?" The Brahmin became uneasy but continued walking. Then the third crook met him and laughed, "What a strange sight. A holy man carrying a dog!" Now the Brahmin was shaken. "If three people have said this, perhaps something is wrong," he thought. Fearing impurity, he threw the goat aside and hurried away. The three crooks collected the goat and laughed at how repetition had defeated clear seeing.
content.source_name: The Panchatantra, translated by Arthur W. Ryder (1925)

content.story_title: How the Crow-Hen Killed the Black Snake
content.book: Book 1, The Loss of Friends (inserted tale)
content.text: In a certain region grew a great banyan tree. In it lived a crow and his wife, occupying the nest which they had built. But a black snake crawled through the hollow trunk and ate their chicks as fast as they were born. At last the crow-hen fell at her husband's feet and said: "My dear lord, a great many children of mine have been eaten by that awful snake... Let us make our home in some other tree." At this the crow was dreadfully depressed, and he said: "We have lived in this tree a long time, my dear. We cannot desert it... Moreover, by some shrewd device I will bring death upon this villainous and mighty foe." After this indignant speech he went at once to another tree, under which lived a dear friend, a jackal. He courteously called the jackal forth, related all his sorrow, then said: "My friend, what do you consider opportune under the circumstances?" "My friend," said the jackal, "tell me how this villainous snake is to meet his doom." And the jackal answered: "Go to some spot frequented by a great monarch. There seize a golden chain or a necklace from some wealthy man who guards it carelessly. Deposit this in such a place that when it is recovered, the snake may be killed." So the crow and his wife straightway flew off at random, and the wife came upon a certain pond. As she looked about, she saw the women of a king's court playing in the water, and on the bank they had laid golden chains, pearl necklaces, garments, and gems. One chain of gold the crow-hen seized and started for the tree where she lived. But when the chamberlains and the eunuchs saw the theft, they picked up clubs and ran in pursuit. Meanwhile, the crow-hen dropped the golden chain in the snake's hole and waited at a safe distance. Now when the king's men climbed the tree, they found a hole and in it a black snake with swelling hood. So they killed him with their clubs, recovered the golden chain, and went their way. Thereafter the crow and his wife lived in peace. And that is why I say: In cases where brute force would fail, A shrewd device may still prevail: The crow-hen used a golden chain, And so the dreadful snake was slain.
content.curated_lesson: A crow and his mate built their nest high in a tree. Below the same tree lived a cobra in a hollow. Whenever the crows laid eggs, the cobra climbed up and ate them. The mother crow wept, and the father crow wanted to fight, but the cobra was too strong. They went to a wise jackal and asked for help. The jackal said, "Do not fight strength with weakness. Use the strength of one power against another. Fly to the palace, take something precious, and drop it near the cobra's hole." The crows flew to the palace garden, where the queen had removed her necklace while bathing. One crow picked it up and flew slowly so the guards could follow. Then he dropped it into the cobra's hollow. The guards reached the tree, saw the necklace inside the hole, and dug it out. When the cobra emerged, they killed it. The crows were safe and raised their young in peace.
content.source_name: The Panchatantra, translated by Arthur W. Ryder (1925)

content.story_title: The Sensible Enemy and the Foolish Friend
content.book: Book 1, The Loss of Friends (inserted tale)
content.text: [...] After a time, as he dallied now and then in the ladies' apartments, he made a pet and constant companion of a monkey from the stable nearby... In course of time the monkey, regaled with a variety of dainties from the royal hand, grew to be a big fellow, and became an object of respect to the entire court. The king, indeed, felt such confidence in the monkey and such affection that he made him his personal sword-bearer. Now the king had near his palace a pleasure-grove... He therefore entered it with his queen in a passion of love, and all his human retinue were left behind at the entrance. After a period of delighted wandering and gazing, the king grew weary and said to the monkey: "I shall rest and sleep a moment in this arbour. You must keep careful watch to prevent anyone from disturbing me." With this he went to sleep. Presently a bee, drawn by the fragrance of flowers, of musk, and other perfumes, hovered over him and alighted on his head. On seeing this, the monkey angrily thought: "What! Under my very eyes this wretched creature looks upon the king!" And he undertook to drive him away. But when the bee, for all his efforts, continued to approach the king, the monkey went blind with rage, drew his sword, and fetched a blow at the bee — a blow that split the king's head. And the queen, who was sleeping beside him, started up in terror, screaming when she beheld the incomprehensible fact: "You fool! You monkey! The king trusted you. How could you do it?" Then the monkey told what had happened, after which everybody, by common consent, scolded him and shunned him. "So there is reason in saying that one should not make friends with a fool, inasmuch as the monkey killed the king. Indeed, that is why I say: To foes of sense, not foolish friends, It's wiser far to cling: The robber for his victims died; The monkey killed the king."
content.curated_lesson: A king had a pet monkey who was devoted to him. The monkey stayed near the king day and night, waving a fan, guarding the room, and trying to serve with complete loyalty. One afternoon, while the king slept, a fly kept landing on his chest. The monkey chased it away again and again, but the fly returned. In anger, the monkey picked up the king's sword. When the fly landed once more, the monkey struck with all his strength. The fly escaped, but the sword wounded the king. The court learned that affection without judgment can be more dangerous than open hostility.
content.source_name: The Panchatantra, translated by Arthur W. Ryder (1925)

content.book: Book 2, The Winning of Friends (inserted tale)
content.text: By a certain lake in the world lived birds called "bharunda birds." They had one belly and two necks apiece. While one of these birds was sauntering about, his first neck found some nectar. Then the second said: "Give me half." And when the first refused, the second neck angrily picked up poison somewhere and ate it. As they had one belly, they died. "And that is why I say: Bharunda birds will teach you why The disunited surely die: For, single-bellied, double-necked, They took a diet incorrect. Thus union is strength."
content.curated_lesson: A rare bird lived near a river. It had two heads but only one body. One day, the first head found a delicious fruit and ate it happily. The second head asked for a share. The first replied, "We have one stomach, so why should it matter who eats?" The second head felt insulted and carried resentment for days. Later, the second head found a poisonous fruit and ate it deliberately. The poison entered the one body they shared, and both heads died. Division within one body destroys all.
content.source_name: The Panchatantra, translated by Arthur W. Ryder (1925)

content.book: Book 1, The Loss of Friends (inserted tale)
content.text: In a great lake lived three full-grown fishes, whose names were Forethought, Ready-wit, and Fatalist. Now one day the fish named Forethought overheard passers-by on the bank and fishermen saying: "There are plenty of fish in this pond. Tomorrow we go fishing." On hearing this, Forethought reflected: "This looks bad. Tomorrow or the day after they will be sure to come here. I will take Ready-wit and Fatalist and move to another lake whose waters are not troubled." So he called them and put the question. Thereupon Ready-wit said: "I have lived long in this lake and cannot move in such a hurry. If fishermen come here, then I will protect myself by some means devised for the occasion." But poor, doomed Fatalist said: "There are sizable lakes elsewhere. Who knows whether they will come here or not? One should not abandon the lake of his birth merely because of such small gossip... Therefore I am determined not to go." And when Forethought realized that their minds were made up, he went to another body of water. On the next day, when he had gone, the fishermen with their boys beset the inner pool, cast a net, and caught all the fish without exception. Under these circumstances Ready-wit, while still in the water, played dead. And since they thought: "This big fellow died without help," they drew him from the net and laid him on the bank, from which he wriggled back to safety in the water. But Fatalist stuck his nose into the meshes of the net, struggling until they pounded him repeatedly with clubs and so killed him. "And that is why I say: Forethought and Ready-wit thrive; Fatalist can't keep alive."
content.curated_lesson: Three fish lived in a pond. When fishermen planned to return with nets, the first fish left through a channel that very night. The second waited but stayed alert. When caught, he pretended to be dead and escaped when thrown aside. The third laughed at fear and did nothing. The third was taken. The story remembers three responses to danger: preparation, adaptability, and denial.
content.source_name: The Panchatantra, translated by Arthur W. Ryder (1925)

content.text: There was once a laundryman named Clean-Cloth in a certain town. He had a single donkey who had grown very feeble from lack of fodder. As the laundryman wandered in the forest, he saw a dead tiger, and he thought: "Ah, this is lucky. I will put this tiger-skin on the donkey and let him loose in the barley fields at night. For the farmers will think him a tiger and will not drive him out." When this was done, the donkey ate barley to his heart's content. And at dawn the laundryman took him back to the barn. So as time passed, he grew plump. He could hardly squeeze into the stall. But one day the donkey heard the bray of a she-donkey in the distance. At the mere sound he himself began to bray. Then the farmers perceived that he was a donkey in disguise, and killed him with blows from clubs and stones and arrows. "And that is why I say: However skilful in disguise, However frightful to the eyes, Although in tiger-skin arrayed, The ass was killed — because he brayed."
content.curated_lesson: A washerman covered his donkey with a tiger skin and let it graze in other people's fields at night. The farmers saw the shape and ran away in fear. The donkey enjoyed the rich crops and grew fat. But one night he heard another donkey braying from far away. Forgetting the disguise, he brayed loudly in answer. The farmers recognised him at once. A borrowed appearance cannot hide one's nature for long.
content.source_name: The Panchatantra, translated by Arthur W. Ryder (1925)

content.story_title: The Mice That Set Elephants Free
content.book: Book 2, The Winning of Friends (inserted tale)
content.text: There was once a region where people, houses, and temples had fallen into decay. So the mice, who were old settlers there, occupied the chinks in the floors of stately dwellings... But into this scene burst an elephant-king, whose retinue numbered thousands. He, with his herd, had started for the lake upon information that there was water there. As he marched through the mouse community, he crushed faces, eyes, heads, and necks of such mice as he encountered. Then the survivors held a convention. "We are being killed," they said, "by these lumbering elephants... Therefore let us devise a remedy effective in this crisis." When they had done so, a certain number went to the lake, bowed before the elephant-king, and said respectfully: "O King, not far from here is our community, inherited from a long line of ancestors... Now you gentlemen, while coming here to water, have destroyed us by the thousand... If then you feel compassion toward us, pray travel another path." And the elephant-king turned over in his mind what he had heard, decided that the statement of the mice was entirely logical, and granted their request. Now in the course of time a certain king commanded his elephant-trappers to trap elephants. And they constructed a so-called water-trap, caught the king with his herd, three days later dragged him out with a great tackle made of ropes and things, and tied him to stout trees in that very bit of forest. When the trappers had gone, the elephant-king reflected thus: "In what manner, or through whose assistance, shall I be delivered?" Then it occurred to him: "We have no means of deliverance except those mice." So the king sent the mice an exact description of his disastrous position in the trap... When the mice learned the matter, they gathered by the thousand, eager to return the favour shown them, and visited the elephant herd. And seeing king and herd fettered, they gnawed the guy-ropes where they stood, then swarmed up the branches, and by cutting the ropes aloft, set their friends free. "And that is why I say: Make friends, make friends, however strong Or weak they be: Recall the captive elephants That mice set free."
content.curated_lesson: A herd of elephants passed daily through a ruined city where thousands of mice lived. Many mice were crushed under their feet. The mouse king respectfully asked the elephant king to use another path. The elephant king agreed. Some elephants laughed, "What help can mice ever give us?" But the king replied, "No creature is too small for respect." Later hunters trapped the elephants with ropes. The mouse king brought his people, and they gnawed through the ropes. The elephants were freed by those they had once spared.
content.source_name: The Panchatantra, translated by Arthur W. Ryder (1925)

content.text: Here, then, begins Book 3, called "Crows and Owls," which treats of peace, war, and so forth. The first verse runs: Reconciled although he be, Never trust an enemy. For the cave of owls was burned, When the crows with fire returned. [...] Now a rival king, a great owl named Foe-Crusher, had his fortress and his habitation in a mountain cave, and he had an unnumbered retinue of owls. This owl-king cherished a grudge, so that whenever he met a crow in his airings, he killed him and passed on. [...] Then Cloudy bowed low before an ancient, farsighted counsellor of his race... named Live-Strong... And Live-Strong said: "...the present hour demands duplicity... I have discovered a fifth device... You must turn against me, revile me with the hardest-hearted words you can find, smear me with blood... throw me out at the foot of this banyan tree, and depart yourself to Antelope Mountain. And there you must stay with your retinue until by clever planning I win the trust of all the enemy, discover the heart of their fortress, and kill them — for they are blind in the daytime." [...] So the owl-king, informed by his followers, came, beheld with astonishment the scars of many wounds... To this the owl-king agreed, and his efficient caterers daily gave Live-Strong, by special command of the king, the pick of the viands. [...] After these reflections, he dropped each day one fagot from the forest into his own nest, with the ultimate purpose of setting the cave afire. Nor did the owls, poor fools, perceive that he was building up his nest in order to burn them alive. [...] Then at sunrise, when the owls became blind, he hastened away and reported to Cloudy: "My lord and king, I have prepared the enemy's cave for burning. Come with your retainers, each bringing a lighted fagot from the forest, to throw on my nest at the gate of the cave. Thus all your foes will die in torments..." So Cloudy and his followers... seized one lighted fagot apiece in their bills, flew to the gate of the cave, and threw their fagots upon Live-Strong's nest. Then all the owls (being blind in the daytime) [perished]... In this fashion Cloudy exterminated his foes and returned to his old fortress in the banyan tree. [...] Here ends Book 3, called "Crows and Owls," which treats of peace, war, and the other four expedients. The first verse runs: Reconciled although he be, Never trust an enemy. For the cave of owls was burned, When the crows with fire returned.
content.curated_lesson: The owls and crows were enemies. The owls attacked at night, when the crows could not see. Many crows wanted immediate revenge, but their elder advised strategy. One crow pretended to be rejected by his own people and took shelter near the owls. Slowly he gained their trust and learned where they slept during the day. When the time was right, he returned to the crows. They blocked the owls' cave with dry grass and smoke. Strategy succeeded where rage would have failed.
content.source_name: The Panchatantra, translated by Arthur W. Ryder (1925)

content.text: There was once a potter in a certain place. One day he carelessly ran with all his might into the jagged edge of a broken pot, and tumbled. And though the jagged edge tore his forehead, he struggled to his feet, blood streaming over his body. Now as the wound was unskilfully treated, the scar cicatrized horribly. After some time the land was afflicted with famine, and he felt the pinch of hunger. So he joined certain life-guards, went to another country, and became a life-guard. Now the king noticed on his brow the horrible scar from the potsherd, and he thought: "Surely, this man is a great hero. He took a wound in front, on his brow." So he bestowed honours and gifts and the like, regarding him more graciously than all others. [...] Now one day there was a review of picked troops... the king took occasion to say to the potter: "O Prince, what is your name? And what your family? In what battle was this wound printed on your brow?" "Your Majesty," he replied, "by birth I am a potter, and my name is Fight-Firm. This is not a sword-wound. But when I was unsteady with liquor, I was hurrying through a courtyard littered with broken pots, and tumbled over one. Later the scar from the potsherd became a horrible cicatrice." Then the king reflected: "Good heavens! I was taken in by this potter who seemed a prince. Let a cuffing be administered." When this had been done, the potter said: "Your Majesty, do not treat me thus, but witness my adroitness in battle." "No, my friend," said the king, "you may be a treasure-house of all the virtues. Yet you must be gone... Just so you, too, had best decamp before these veterans learn that you are a potter. If not, you will be hooted and killed." And the potter, hearing this, absconded.
content.curated_lesson: A potter once fell on broken pots and received a deep scar on his forehead. Years later, a king saw the scar and assumed he was a brave soldier. The potter was given honour in the army. He enjoyed the respect but did not explain the truth. Then war approached, and the king asked him to lead fighters. Terrified, the potter confessed. The king said, "A scar is not courage." Titles gained by misunderstanding cannot stand before real duty.
content.source_name: The Panchatantra, translated by Arthur W. Ryder (1925)

content.book: Book 3, Crows and Owls (inserted tale, within How the Birds Picked a King)
content.text: At one time I was living in a certain tree. And beneath the same tree dwelt another bird, a partridge... One day the partridge went foraging with other birds to a spot where the rice was ripe and abundant, and he did not return at nightfall... Now one evening a rabbit named Speedy made himself at home in the partridge's old nest in the hole... However, one fine day the partridge, who had grown extremely plump from eating rice, remembered his old home and returned... Now when he saw the rabbit in the hole, he said reproachfully: "Come now, rabbit, you have done a shabby thing in occupying my apartment. Please be gone." "You fool!" said the rabbit, "don't you know that a dwelling is yours only while you occupy it?" "Very well, then," said the partridge, "suppose we ask the neighbours." ... Now they had not travelled far when the rabbit asked the partridge: "My good fellow, who is to pass judgement on our disagreement?" And the partridge answered: "...there dwells a tomcat whose name is Curd-Ear. He abides unshaken in his vow of penance and self-denial." [...] So together they began to question him: "O holy moralist, a dispute has arisen between us. Pray give judgement in accordance with the moral law. And whichever of us is found to speak falsely, him you may eat." "Dear friends," said the cat, "I implore you not to speak thus. My soul abhors every act of cruelty... I shall eat nobody. However, I am somewhat old and do not readily distinguish your voices from a distance... pray draw near and make me acquainted with the case... confide in me and speak clearly at the edge of my ear." Why spin it out? That seedy rogue won their trust so fully that both drew near him. Then, of course, he seized them simultaneously, one with his paw, the other with the saw of his teeth. And when they were dead, he ate them both. "And that is why I say: A seedy umpire is not very Pleasing to either adversary: Rabbit and partridge teach you that — They died, confiding in the cat."
content.curated_lesson: A partridge returned to his old nest and found a hare living there. They argued over ownership and decided to ask someone wise to judge. They saw an old cat sitting like a saint by the river. The cat spoke of non-violence and justice, saying, "Come closer; my ears are weak." Trusting his gentle words, both came near. The cat seized them. The dispute ended not through justice but through choosing a judge without character.
content.source_name: The Panchatantra, translated by Arthur W. Ryder (1925)
